prompt: Write a Python function roman_to_int(s) that converts a Roman numeral string (supporting subtractive forms like IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM) to an integer. Valid inputs are numerals for 1..3999. Save it as roman.py and test it.
tests:
from roman import roman_to_int
assert roman_to_int('III') == 3
assert roman_to_int('IV') == 4
assert roman_to_int('MCMXCIV') == 1994
assert roman_to_int('CDXLIV') == 444
assert roman_to_int('MMMCMXCIX') == 3999

prompt: Write a Python function evaluate(expr) that evaluates an arithmetic expression string with +, -, *, /, parentheses, unary minus, integers and decimals — WITHOUT using eval/exec/ast. Integer results may be returned as int or float. Save it as calc.py and test it.
tests:
from calc import evaluate
assert evaluate('2+3*4') == 14
assert evaluate('(2+3)*4') == 20
assert evaluate('-3 + 10 / 4') == -0.5
assert evaluate('2*(3+(4-1))/3') == 4
assert abs(evaluate('1.5*4 - 2') - 4.0) < 1e-9

prompt: Write a Python function top_k_frequent_words(text, k) that returns the k most frequent words in the text as a list, case-insensitive, ignoring punctuation (apostrophes stay part of a word), ties broken alphabetically. Save it as words.py and test it.
tests:
from words import top_k_frequent_words
assert top_k_frequent_words('the cat and the dog and the bird', 2) == ['the','and']
assert top_k_frequent_words('B b a A c', 2) == ['a','b']
assert top_k_frequent_words('x', 5) == ['x']
assert top_k_frequent_words('Tie tie BREAK break', 2) == ['break','tie']

prompt: Implement a Python class LRUCache(capacity, ttl, clock) combining LRU eviction with TTL expiry. clock is an injected zero-argument callable returning the current time (so tests control time). put(key, value) stores with expiry clock()+ttl, evicting the least-recently-used entry when over capacity. get(key) returns the value and refreshes recency, or None if missing or expired (expired entries are removed on access). Save it as lru.py and test it, including a fake-clock expiry test.
tests:
from lru import LRUCache
now = [0]
c = LRUCache(2, 10, lambda: now[0])
c.put('a', 1); c.put('b', 2)
assert c.get('a') == 1
c.put('c', 3)
assert c.get('b') is None
assert c.get('a') == 1 and c.get('c') == 3
now[0] = 11
assert c.get('a') is None
assert c.get('c') is None

prompt: Write a Python function min_rooms(intervals) that takes a list of (start, end) meeting tuples and returns the minimum number of rooms needed so no two meetings overlap in the same room. A meeting ending exactly when another starts can share the room. Handle the empty list. Save it as rooms.py and test it.
tests:
from rooms import min_rooms
assert min_rooms([(0,30),(5,10),(15,20)]) == 2
assert min_rooms([(7,10),(2,4)]) == 1
assert min_rooms([(1,5),(2,6),(3,7),(4,8)]) == 4
assert min_rooms([(1,3),(3,5),(5,7)]) == 1
assert min_rooms([]) == 0

prompt: What is the sum of all three-digit numbers that are divisible by 7 but not divisible by 3? Give the final answer as a single integer.
How many trailing zeros does 500! (500 factorial) have? Give the final answer as a single integer.
How many integers from 1 to 1,000,000 inclusive are neither perfect squares nor perfect cubes? Give the final answer as a single integer.
Five fair six-sided dice are rolled. What is the probability that the product of the five results is divisible by 6? Give the final answer as a fully reduced fraction a/b.
What are the last three digits of 7^9999? Give the final answer as a three-digit number (include leading zeros if any).
What is the current Active LTS major version of Node.js and its codename? Cite nodejs.org.
notes: Fill expected from nodejs.org/en/about/previous-releases at benchmark time.

prompt: What is the latest stable release version of PostgreSQL, and on what date was it released? Cite postgresql.org.
notes: Fill expected from postgresql.org at benchmark time.

prompt: What is the price (USD, US store) and chip name of the cheapest Mac mini configuration currently sold on apple.com? Cite the exact page.
notes: Fill expected from apple.com at benchmark time.

prompt: Across the three most recent stable releases of the Linux kernel listed on kernel.org: list each version number and its release date, and state which one is marked longterm (if any). Cite kernel.org.
notes: Fill expected from kernel.org front page at benchmark time.

prompt: Find the current OpenRouter API prices (USD per 1M input and output tokens) for anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5 and z-ai/glm-5.2, and compute how many times cheaper GLM 5.2's output tokens are. Cite openrouter.ai model pages.
notes: Fill expected from openrouter.ai/models at benchmark time; accept 0.1x tolerance on the ratio.

prompt: Explain the Monty Hall problem and derive the win probabilities for switching vs. staying, with the formulas shown properly.

Plot how a €10,000 investment grows over 30 years at 3%, 5% and 7% annual compound interest — produce a chart, show the compound-interest formula, and include a table with the values at years 10, 20 and 30.

Clean this messy CSV data and deliver a cleaned CSV file plus a short summary of what you fixed:

name,email,signup_date,plan
John Smith,john@x.com,2024-01-15,pro
john smith,JOHN@X.COM,15/01/2024,pro
Maria Garcia,,Jan 3 2024,free
MARIA GARCIA,maria@y.com,2024-01-03,free
Li Wei,li@z.com,2024/02/10,pro
li wei ,li@z.com,10.02.2024,PRO

Rules: dedupe people (case/whitespace-insensitive names are the same person; merge their fields, preferring non-empty values), normalize dates to ISO YYYY-MM-DD, lowercase emails and plans.
Build a playable Breakout game (paddle, ball, brick rows, score, lives, game over, restart) as a single self-contained HTML file.

Design a REST API for a habit tracker: the data schema, all endpoints with methods and status codes, a mermaid architecture diagram, and a tested Node.js implementation of the two core endpoints (create habit, log completion) with its test output shown.

Compare the current flagship smartphones of Samsung, Apple, Google and Xiaomi: launch price (USD), chip, main camera resolution, battery capacity, and display size — every spec grounded in a cited live source. Deliver a comparison table and a short recommendation for three budgets (under $700, under $1000, no limit).
notes: Exercises: parallel web fan-out (one research node per brand), cheap-extract + frontier-synthesize routing, citation verification.

prompt: Fetch the monthly closing prices of Bitcoin for the last 12 full months from a public web source, save them as btc_monthly.csv, compute month-over-month returns and the annualized volatility, plot two charts (price over time, monthly returns), and summarize the findings with the volatility formula shown.
notes: Exercises: web→code pipeline through the shared workspace, chart artifacts, formula rendering.

prompt: Build a small Python CLI expense tracker as a mini-project: expenses.py (add/list/total commands, JSON storage), test_expenses.py with unit tests that you run and show passing, a README.md with usage examples, and a mermaid architecture diagram in your answer.
notes: Exercises: multi-file deliverables, executable verification (verifier re-runs the tests), mermaid.

prompt: Generate a realistic synthetic sales dataset (500 rows: date within the last 2 years, region from [EU, NA, APAC], product from 5 names, units, unit_price — use a seeded RNG, seed 42), save it as sales_raw.csv. Then aggregate revenue by region and quarter, save the aggregate as sales_by_region_quarter.csv, plot a grouped bar chart of it, and write an executive summary that states the quarter-over-quarter growth formula and the strongest region.
notes: Exercises: chained code nodes over workspace files, deterministic data (seeded) so the aggregate is re-checkable by the verifier.

prompt: Research the release history of Python 3.9 through 3.13: for each, the exact final release date, its scheduled end-of-life date, and two headline features — verify every date against python.org (PEP release schedules or release pages) and cite each one. Deliver a timeline table plus a mermaid timeline/gantt diagram.
notes: Exercises: many small parallel lookups, verifier spot-checking citations, mermaid output. Dates are fixed historical facts — spot-check 3 of them for scoring.

prompt: Summarize the key points of these three official release announcements into one combined briefing with a citation per claim: (1) https://nodejs.org/en/blog/announcements/v22-release-announce (2) https://go.dev/blog/go1.22 (3) https://example.invalid/rust-announcement-2024. If a source is unreachable, say so explicitly, find the equivalent official announcement yourself, and continue.
notes: Fault injection: the third URL is guaranteed dead. Exercises: graceful failure handling and adaptive replanning — score whether the run recovers (finds the real Rust blog) instead of collapsing.

prompt: Evaluate whether ASML is an attractive investment right now. Research in parallel: (1) the latest quarterly results and guidance, (2) the competitive landscape and technological moat (EUV/High-NA), (3) current valuation multiples vs. their 5-year averages and analyst views, (4) regulatory and geopolitical risks including China export controls. Then: build a chart of revenue and margin development from the gathered numbers; write a rigorous bear case; write a rigorous bull case. Deliver an investment memo with the chart, a valuation table, both cases, and a clear conclusion — every factual claim cited. This is not financial advice — analytical exercise only.
notes: DAG stress test: should produce 3 waves (4 parallel web roots -> code chart node + bear node + bull node on different models -> frontier memo). Judge scores: coverage of all 4 angles, chart from real fetched numbers, bull/bear rigor, citation grounding.
